aliases: Ground meat and pine nut fritters

english: Ground meat and pine nut fritters

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for kibbeh exists; closest_parent 'meat-based fried food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific kibbeh entry; A0CKW 'Meat products, cooked' is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for kibbeh identified; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fried kibbeh consists of approximately 3 oval or round balls weighing about 150 g in total, often served with yoghurt dip or fresh salad.

nutrient_source.title: Kibbeh (fried, lamb and bulgur) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry for fried kibbeh balls exists. Values are derived from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked ground lamb (NDB 17066), bulgur wheat cooked (NDB 20014), and pine nuts (NDB 12147), combined in proportions consistent with standard kibbeh recipes (approximately 50% ground meat, 35% bulgur, 10% pine nuts, 5% onion/spices) and adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (~8% added fat). This is an adapted/estimated composite. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bulgur wheat and mixed meat-grain dishes — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried kibbeh balls was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on the GI of bulgur wheat (GI ~46, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source, adjusted downward for the high protein and fat content of the dish which slows gastric emptying and reduces glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (41 × 17.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 10.6 g. This is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~465 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kibbeh portion size to one or two balls and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salty dips, as a standard serving contributes approximately 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~10.6 per serving) from bulgur wheat carbohydrate, partially offset by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried kibbeh has a low glycemic index (~41) due to bulgur wheat and high protein and fat content, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~10.6); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in controlled portions and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g) and deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried kibbeh contains moderate saturated fat from lamb and deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider baked or grilled kibbeh preparations to reduce total and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kibbeh (also spelled kibbe, kubba, or kubbeh) is a traditional Levantine dish widely eaten across Turkey, Lebanon, Syria, and the broader Middle East. The classic fried version consists of oval or round torpedo-shaped shells made from a mixture of fine bulgur wheat, ground lamb or beef, and spices (allspice, cinnamon, black pepper), encasing a filling of coarser ground meat sautéed with onions, toasted pine kernels, and warm spices. The assembled balls are deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is crisp and golden-brown, while the interior remains moist and aromatic. The texture contrasts a crunchy outer crust with a tender, spiced meat-and-grain interior. Kibbeh is a protein-rich, moderate-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index (~41) attributable to the intact grain structure of bulgur wheat and the high protein and fat content that slows digestion. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 231 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat, and 17.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is moderate (~310 mg/100 g) and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. The saturated fat contribution from lamb and frying oil is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore, kibbeh is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants and is typically presented as a starter or sharing plate of 3–6 balls accompanied by yoghurt dip, hummus, or tabbouleh. It is halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts.

confidence_reason: No exact kibbeh entry was found in a primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central component data for ground lamb, bulgur wheat, and pine nuts combined in standard recipe proportions and adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption. GI is an analogous estimate based on bulgur wheat GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved-source entry directly matches fried kibbeh balls.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ground lamb (NDB 17066), cooked bulgur wheat (NDB 20014), and pine nuts (NDB 12147), blended in approximate recipe proportions and adjusted for ~8% fat absorption during deep-frying. A direct laboratory analysis or a primary database entry for kibbeh would improve confidence to high. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA component sources used for this composite estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence. 4. GI of 41 is an analogous estimate based on bulgur wheat GI (~46) from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the protein and fat matrix of the dish; a direct GI measurement for kibbeh is not available in the database. 5. The dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore (field survey source). 6. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Turkish/Lebanese halal cuisine); kosher status is not confirmed. 7. Chinese names (基贝 / 基貝) are phonetic transliterations; no established Chinese culinary term for kibbeh exists in standard usage.
